formFactors.name: SFP
formFactors.notes: Small Form-factor Pluggable. Original 1G transceiver standard (SFF-8472). LC Duplex for optical variants; RJ45 for copper. Still widely deployed on access switches, SONET/SDH equipment, and legacy enterprise gear. SFP modules fit in SFP+ ports at 1G rates on most switch vendors.

formFactors.name: SFP+
formFactors.notes: Enhanced SFP for 10G (SFF-8431). Same mechanical form factor as SFP but electrically distinct. LC Duplex connector on all optical variants. The dominant 10G form factor for access, metro, and enterprise. Backward-compatible SFP modules work in SFP+ ports at 1G on most equipment.

formFactors.name: SFP28
formFactors.notes: SFP for 25G (SFF-8402). Same mechanical form factor as SFP/SFP+. LC Duplex. Used in 25G server-to-switch and carrier aggregation deployments. Increasingly common in new data center and carrier infrastructure as 25G replaces 10G as the standard server NIC speed.

formFactors.name: QSFP+
formFactors.notes: Quad SFP+. Four 10G lanes = 40G aggregate. MPO-12 for parallel (SR4) variants. LC Duplex for WDM (LR4) variants. Dominant 40G form factor. Can break out to 4× 10G using MPO-to-4×LC breakout cables. Being phased out in favor of QSFP28 in new builds.

formFactors.name: QSFP28
formFactors.notes: Quad SFP28. Four 25G lanes = 100G aggregate. MPO-12 for parallel (SR4) variants. LC Duplex for WDM variants. The dominant 100G form factor for data centers and metro. Can break out to 4× 25G using MPO-to-4×LC breakout cables. QSFP-DD ports are backward compatible with QSFP28 modules.

formFactors.name: QSFP-DD
formFactors.notes: Double-Density QSFP. Eight 50G PAM4 lanes = 400G aggregate. MPO-16 for parallel (SR8) variants. LC Duplex or MPO-12 for WDM variants. The dominant 400G form factor for hyperscale data centers and new carrier metro deployments. Backward compatible with QSFP28 modules in QSFP-DD ports.

formFactors.name: OSFP
formFactors.notes: Octal SFP. Competing 400G/800G standard to QSFP-DD. Slightly wider form factor — NOT backward compatible with QSFP-DD ports. Used by select switch vendors for 400G and 800G. MPO-16 or LC depending on variant. Verify port type carefully before ordering — OSFP and QSFP-DD are not interchangeable.

transceivers.fieldNotes: 550 m on OM2/OM3/OM4. 275 m on OM1 (62.5/125 µm). VCSEL source at 850 nm. Common on legacy campus switches, storage HBAs, and 1G access gear. Straightforward to test with standard multimode test equipment.

10 km on OS2. On multimode fiber, a Mode Conditioning Patch Cable (MCP) is required — the SMF-pigtailed DFB laser will cause modal noise on OM1/OM2 without it. Without MCP on MMF, the link fails intermittently due to optical interference. Always verify whether an MCP is in place before troubleshooting LX links on MMF plant.

70–80 km on OS2. High TX power — never look into a powered port or connector. The receiver saturates on short fiber — an in-line optical attenuator is required on links under approximately 10 km. This is not an IEEE standard protocol — vendor-specific implementation, behavior may vary between manufacturers.

Single-fiber BiDi. D side transmits 1490 nm and receives 1310 nm. Must pair with BX10-U (transmits 1310 nm, receives 1490 nm) on the far end. Use only single-strand (simplex) jumpers. Verify polarity — two D or two U ends on the same link produce no light.

300 m on OM3, 400 m on OM4. The most common 10G optic in data centers. Low cost and widely available. No SMF option — VCSEL-only. For 10G over SMF, use LR. Narrow power budget — verify loss is under 2.6 dB on long runs before assuming it will link up.

10 km on OS2. Standard 10G SFP+ for OSP access and metro links. Never plug directly into a very short fiber (under 2 m) — the high TX power will saturate the receiver. On any link under 2 km, verify the RX power is within the receiver's range using DOM data or a power meter.

40 km on OS2. EML (Electro-absorptive Modulated Laser) source — more thermally sensitive than DFB lasers. On links shorter than about 10 km, an in-line attenuator is required to avoid saturating the receiver. Verify DOM RX power before assuming the link is healthy.

80 km on OS2. High TX power and very sensitive receiver — the power management window is narrow. Attenuator almost always required on short links. Not an IEEE standard (IEEE 802.3ae defines 10G only to 40 km as ER) — this is a vendor-extended specification. Interoperability between different vendors' ZR modules is not guaranteed.

70 m on OM3, 100 m on OM4/OM5. Very short reach — primary application is server-to-top-of-rack switch in data centers. Very narrow power budget (0 dB loss allowance at minimum) — keep fiber runs short, connectors clean, and verify DOM RX power on every link.
10 km on OS2. The 25G equivalent of 10GBASE-LR for metro and carrier aggregation upgrades. Increasingly deployed as carriers transition access aggregation from 10G to 25G per-lane.

100 m on OM3, 150 m on OM4. Four parallel 850 nm VCSEL lanes over MPO-12 ribbon fiber. MPO polarity management is critical — Type A, B, and C polarity schemes are not interchangeable. A wrong polarity jumper will cross-connect 4 independent channels, creating a link that physically connects but transmits garbage. Verify polarity with a visual polarity tester before installation.

nerdStuff.title: MPO Polarity Types
nerdStuff.explanation: MPO fiber assemblies have 12 (MPO-12) or 16 (MPO-16) fibers in a single rectangular connector. TIA-568 defines three polarity types (A, B, C) that determine which fiber position at one end maps to which position at the other end. Type A is a straight-through (position 1 to position 1). Type B is a flipped pair (position 1 to position 12). Type C uses a pair-wise flip. For parallel optics (SR4, SR8), the transmit lanes from one transceiver must map to the receive lanes of the other. Getting polarity wrong produces a physical link with no data flow. Always document polarity type when installing MPO infrastructure.

transceivers.fieldNotes: 10 km on OS2 over duplex LC. Four CWDM wavelengths (1271/1291/1311/1331 nm) multiplexed on 2 fibers. Simplex LC — no MPO required. Easier to deploy than SR4 on existing SMF OSP plant. Can break out to 4× 10G using a passive CWDM demultiplexer — check vendor compatibility before planning a breakout design.

70 m on OM3, 100 m on OM4/OM5. Four parallel 850 nm lanes over MPO-12. High-density data center interconnect — the dominant 100G MMF solution. MPO polarity management is critical (same issue as 40G-SR4). Verify polarity with a tester before installation. Can break out to 4× 25G SR with MPO-to-4×LC breakout cables.

10 km on OS2. Four WDM channels near 1310 nm over duplex LC. The standard 100G solution for metro DCI and data center interconnect over existing SMF plant. LC Duplex — no MPO required. Chromatic dispersion adds ~1 dB penalty at 10 km — include dispersion penalty in link budget for spans over 5 km.

40 km on OS2. Extended reach 100G for metro and long-haul DCI. Higher TX power than LR4 — requires attenuator on links shorter than approximately 10 km. Dispersion compensation may be needed on spans above 20 km depending on the system's dispersion budget.

500 m on OS2 over MPO-12. Four parallel 1310 nm PAM4 lanes. Very tight power budget — designed for short-reach intra-campus or inter-building runs on existing SMF. Can break out to 4× 100G LR using MPO-to-LC duplex breakout cables. MPO polarity still applies — verify before installing.

nerdStuff.title: PAM4 vs NRZ — Why 400G Is Different to Test
nerdStuff.explanation: Early generations of optical transceivers (up to 100G per lane on some systems) used NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) modulation — each symbol represents one bit (0 or 1). 400G per-lane and high-speed 100G use PAM4 (4-level Pulse Amplitude Modulation) where each symbol represents 2 bits using 4 amplitude levels. PAM4 doubles data throughput per wavelength but is significantly more sensitive to noise, dispersion, and reflections than NRZ. PAM4 links have a much tighter power budget and are more sensitive to dirty connectors — a connector that passes on a 10G NRZ link may cause BER errors on a 400G PAM4 link. Inspect connectors to IEC 61300-3-35 Grade B or better.

transceivers.fieldNotes: 2 km on OS2 over duplex LC. Four CWDM wavelengths. DCI and inter-building 400G over existing duplex SMF plant. LC Duplex simplifies deployment versus MPO-based parallel optics.
10 km on OS2 over duplex LC. Four CWDM wavelengths. Long-reach 400G for metro and campus. The 400G equivalent of 100GBASE-LR4 — allows 400G over existing duplex SMF infrastructure.

generalFieldNotes: Never look into a powered fiber connector or transceiver port. Even low-power 1G SFP optics can cause permanent retinal damage. Use power meters, visual fault locators, or fiber inspection scopes only.

Short fiber on high-power transceivers (ZR, ER) will saturate the receiver and cause link errors or physical damage to the detector. Insert a 5–15 dB inline optical attenuator when TX power exceeds the receiver's max input.

BiDi transceivers (BX, single-fiber) transmit and receive on different wavelengths on the same fiber strand. They must be paired correctly: D-end ↔ U-end. Two matching ends on the same link produce no light — a common miscabling error.

MPO-based parallel optics (SR4, SR8, DR4) require polarity management. Type A, B, and C MPO polarity schemes are not interchangeable. Verify polarity with a visual polarity tester before terminating jumpers — a wrong polarity jumper will cause no link even with perfect signal levels.

Transceivers are vendor-locked in many switches and routers. A generic third-party module may fail an EEPROM compatibility check and refuse to link up. Check vendor compatibility before ordering — or use the vendor's own optics if the project budget allows.

DOM (Digital Optical Monitoring) data — TX power, RX power, temperature, supply voltage, TX bias current — is readable via switch CLI (show interfaces / show optics / show transceiver). Always pull DOM data before and after troubleshooting a failing optical link.

Cap all disconnected transceiver ports and fiber ends immediately after disconnecting. Dust on an LC, SC, or MPO end face accumulates within seconds in a data center environment and is the leading cause of optical link failure on new installations.
